meta.title: ICH Q5 Series — Biologics Quality Requirements (Verbatim-Verified)
meta.guideline: ICH Q5 Series — Biologics Quality Requirements
meta.extraction_notes: All source_quote fields are verbatim text extracted from the respective PDF pages as rendered in the source documents.

rules.rule_text: Extensive screening for both endogenous viruses and adventitious viral contamination must be performed on the Master Cell Bank (MCB).
rules.source_quote: Extensive screening for both endogenous viruses and adventitious viral contamination should be performed on the MCB. For hetero-hybrid cell lines, for example, in which one or more partners are human or non-human primate in origin, tests should be performed to detect viruses of human or non-human primate origin because viral contamination arising from these cells may pose a particular risk.

rules.rule_text: Testing for adventitious viruses on the MCB must include both broad and specific virus detection assays, including NGS where appropriate.
rules.source_quote: Testing for adventitious viruses should include both broad and specific virus detection assays as described in Table 1. Introduction of new methodologies such as NGS for detecting a broad range of adventitious viruses is encouraged. To ensure detection of contaminating viruses, the testing approach should be based on a risk assessment, considering the origin and history of the parental cell line and the potential exposure to materials of human or animal origin during the generation of production cells and banking of the MCB.

rules.rule_text: Each Working Cell Bank (WCB) must be tested for adventitious viruses as described in Table 1 of Q5A(R2).
rules.source_quote: Each WCB should be tested for adventitious viruses as described in Table 1. When appropriate, if adventitious virus tests have been performed on the MCB, and cells cultured up to or beyond the LIVCA have been derived from the WCB and tested for the presence of adventitious viruses, then similar tests may be omitted from this WCB.

rules.rule_text: Cells at the Limit of In Vitro Cell Age (LIVCA) must be evaluated once for viruses that may have been undetected in the MCB and/or WCB.
rules.source_quote: Cells at the LIVCA should be evaluated once for viruses that may have been undetected in the MCB and/or WCB. The performance of suitable tests (as outlined in Table 1) once on cells at the LIVCA on production will provide further assurance that the production process does not lead to induction of endogenous viruses, reactivation of latent viruses or amplification of low level or slow growing viruses.

rules.rule_text: Cell lines must be characterised for retroviruses; tests must be performed on the MCB and cells cultured up to or beyond the LIVCA, and may include infectivity assays, RT activity assays, TEM, and cocultivation.
rules.source_quote: Cell lines should be characterised for the presence of retroviruses. Tests for retroviruses should be performed on the MCB and cells cultured up to or beyond the LIVCA used for production. These tests may include infectivity assays by direct inoculation of the cell supernatant or cocultivation of the cells, assays for reverse transcriptase (RT) activity using cell-free supernatant, and evaluation of the cells for particles by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM).

rules.rule_text: For cell lines producing retroviral particles (rodent, insect, avian), TEM must be performed to examine the type of retroviral particles present; infectivity assays must be performed to determine whether endogenous retroviral particles are infectious.
rules.source_quote: If a cell line is producing retroviral particles (as occurs in some cell lines derived from rodent, insect, and avian species), RT activity is expected and therefore a PCR-based RT assay (e.g., product-enhanced Reverse Transcriptase (PERT) assay) may not be needed. TEM should be performed to examine the type of retroviral particles (e.g., type-A and type-C) present. To determine whether the endogenous retroviral particles are infectious, infectivity assays should be performed using relevant permissive cells (e.g., Mus dunni for general murine retrovirus detection, SC-1 cells for ecotropic murine retrovirus detection) with sensitive readout assays for retrovirus detection (e.g., PERT assay, a Sarcoma-Positive, Leukaemia-Negative (S+L-) assay, or an XC plaque assay).

rules.rule_text: For in vitro cell culture infectivity assays (MCB, WCB, LIVCA qualification), 28-day testing on permissive cells must be performed with at least one sub-passage at two weeks.
rules.source_quote: For qualification of the MCB, WCB, and LIVCA, 28-day testing on permissive cells should be performed with at least one sub-passage at two weeks.

rules.rule_text: The in vitro infectivity assay panel must include a cell line of the species of origin, human diploid cells (e.g., MRC-5), and a monkey kidney cell line (e.g., Vero).
rules.source_quote: The panel of indicator cell lines should include a cell line of the species of origin, human diploid cells (e.g., MRC-5) and a monkey kidney cell line (e.g., Vero). Additional cell lines may be included. The choice of cells used in the test should be based on a risk assessment considering the species of origin of the cell substrate to be tested.

rules.rule_text: The indicator cell cultures in the in vitro assay must be monitored for cytopathic viruses, hemadsorbing, and hemagglutinating viruses, consistent with existing regional regulations and guidance.
rules.source_quote: The indicator cell cultures should be monitored for cytopathic viruses, hemadsorbing and hemagglutinating viruses, consistent with existing regional regulations and guidance.

rules.rule_text: In vivo testing is not necessary for extensively used well-characterised cell lines such as CHO, NS0, and SP2/0, based on prior knowledge.
rules.source_quote: In vivo testing may be performed based on risk assessment considering the history and manufacturing of the cell bank and the testing strategy. However, in vivo testing is not necessary for extensively used well-characterised cell lines such as CHO, NS0 and SP/2,0 based on prior knowledge.

rules.rule_text: Non-targeted NGS is encouraged as a replacement for in vivo assays due to breadth and sensitivity of virus detection, and to reduce, replace, and refine use of animals.
rules.source_quote: Non-targeted NGS is encouraged as a replacement for in vivo assays due to its breadth and sensitivity of virus detection and the limitations of the in vivo assays. Furthermore, this promotes the global initiative to replace, reduce, and refine the use of animal testing.

rules.rule_text: The list of specific viruses to test for must be defined based on a viral contamination risk assessment considering the origin of cells and potential sources of viral contamination including biological raw materials.
rules.source_quote: The list of specific viruses to be tested for is defined based on a viral contamination risk assessment and considers (but is not limited to) the origin of the cells and the potential sources of viral contamination (e.g., biological raw materials, especially those of human or animal origin). For cell lines with exposure to human or animal derived raw materials, (e.g., bovine serum and porcine trypsin), tests for human, bovine and porcine viruses should be performed.

rules.rule_text: For cell lines of rodent origin or exposed to rodent materials, Nucleic Acid Amplification techniques (NAT) or antibody production tests in mice, rats, or hamsters must be used to detect species-specific viruses.
rules.source_quote: For cell lines of rodent origin or exposure to rodent materials, Nucleic Acid Amplification techniques (NAT) or antibody production tests in mice, rats or hamsters are used to detect species-specific viruses. These tests should be performed when the potential exists for exposure to viruses of a specific animal species.

rules.rule_text: NAT such as PCR assays or targeted or non-targeted NGS may be used for replacing the rodent antibody production animal assays (MAP, RAP, HAP) without a head-to-head comparison.
rules.source_quote: NAT such as PCR assays or targeted or non-targeted NGS or other molecular methods can be used for replacing the animal assays described in Table 3, without head-to-head comparison.

rules.rule_text: NGS methods used for virus detection must have validation/qualification provided to support their intended use, including method validation and matrix-specific verification when used as replacement methods.
rules.source_quote: For any NGS method used, validation/qualification should be provided to support its intended use for the application. This includes method validation and matrix-specific verification when used as a replacement method. When used as a supplementary method, this includes method qualification and matrix-specific verification. Method validation requires predefined performance criteria while method qualification only evaluates the performance characteristics of the method.

rules.rule_text: Manufacturers must develop programs to routinely test for adventitious viruses in production batches of unprocessed bulk.
rules.source_quote: It is recommended that manufacturers develop programs to routinely test for adventitious viruses in production batches. The extent of virus testing on the unprocessed bulk should consider several points including the nature of the cell lines used to produce the desired products, the results and extent of virus tests performed during the qualification of the cell lines, the cultivation method, the raw material and reagent sources, and the results of viral clearance studies.

rules.rule_text: Adventitious virus testing must be routinely applied to each unprocessed bulk, including in vitro assays using several indicator cell lines observed for 28 days with at least one sub-passage at two weeks.
rules.source_quote: Adventitious virus testing should be routinely applied to each unprocessed bulk. This testing may include in vitro assays using several indicator cell lines or non-targeted NGS (see Section 3.2). The indicator cell cultures should be observed for 28 days including at least one sub-passage at two weeks. Assay duration may be reduced with justification to 14 days for cell lines based on a risk assessment (considering the cell substrate, cultivation period for production, use of animal-derived raw materials or reagents, and level of viral clearance of the process).

rules.rule_text: If adventitious viruses are detected at the unprocessed bulk stage, the product harvest must not be used for product manufacture unless justified.
rules.source_quote: If any adventitious viruses are detected at the unprocessed bulk stage, the product harvest from cell culture should not be used for product manufacture unless justified. (See Section 5 for guidance on the use of material in which an adventitious virus has been detected in the harvest material.) The process should be carefully checked to determine the root cause and extent of the contamination, and appropriate actions should be taken.

rules.rule_text: Characterisation of clearance using non-specific model viruses must be performed in all cases (Cases A through F) in the action plan for viral clearance studies.
rules.source_quote: In all cases, characterisation of clearance using non-specific "model" viruses should be performed. The most common situations are Cases A, B and F. Production systems contaminated with a virus other than a rodent retrovirus normally are not used. When there are well-justified reasons for drug production using a cell line from Cases C, D, or E, these should be discussed with the appropriate regulatory authorities.

rules.rule_text: For rodent cell lines (Case B), RVLP quantification data from 3 lots or more of purified bulk at pilot plant scale or commercial scale must typically be provided for marketing authorisation.
rules.source_quote: For marketing authorisation, typically RVLP quantification data from 3 lots or more of purified bulk at pilot plant scale or commercial scale should be provided. Well-characterised cell lines such as CHO, C127, BHK and murine hybridoma cell lines (e.g., NS0 or SP2/0 cells) have frequently been used as substrates for drug production with no reported safety problems related to viral contamination of the products.

rules.rule_text: When a virus infectious to humans (Case D) is identified, the product may be considered acceptable only under exceptional circumstances, with the process required to remove and inactivate the virus during downstream processing.
rules.source_quote: If a virus infectious to humans (such as those viruses indicated in Table 3, footnote a), is identified, the product should be considered acceptable only under exceptional circumstances. In such instances, the identified virus should be used for virus removal and inactivation evaluation studies and specific methods with high specificity and sensitivity for the detection of the virus in question should be used.

rules.rule_text: When a virus that cannot be classified by current methodologies is detected in cells or unprocessed bulk (Case E), the product is usually considered unacceptable because the virus may be pathogenic.
rules.source_quote: When a virus that cannot be classified by currently available methodologies is detected in the cells or unprocessed bulk, the product is usually considered unacceptable because the virus may be pathogenic. In the rare case in which there are well-justified reasons for drug production using such a cell line, this should be discussed with the appropriate regulatory authorities before proceeding further.

rules.rule_text: Viral clearance studies must be carried out in a well-documented and controlled manner; the objectives are to (1) assess process steps that effectively inactivate or remove viruses and (2) estimate quantitatively the overall level of virus reduction.
rules.source_quote: Studies should be carried out in a well-documented and controlled manner. The objectives of viral clearance studies are (1) to assess process steps that effectively inactivate or remove viruses; and (2) to estimate quantitatively the overall level of virus reduction obtained by the process. These should be achieved by the deliberate addition (i.e., "spiking") of significant amounts of virus to the unprocessed bulk or to process intermediates obtained during the various process steps and demonstrating its removal or inactivation during the subsequent steps.

rules.rule_text: When determining the amount of virus particles that enter the purification process, quantification must be performed on three cell culture lots/batches, submitted as part of the marketing application.
rules.source_quote: When appropriate, to determine the amount of virus particles that enter the purification process, quantification should be performed on three cell cultures lots/batches. These data should be submitted as part of the marketing application.

rules.rule_text: Viruses for clearance evaluation must be chosen to resemble viruses which may contaminate the product and to represent a wide range of physicochemical properties.
rules.source_quote: Viruses for clearance evaluation and process characterisation studies should be chosen to resemble viruses which may contaminate the product and to represent a wide range of physicochemical properties to test the ability of the system to eliminate viruses in general. The manufacturer should justify the choice of viruses according to the aims of the evaluation and characterisation study provided in this guideline.

rules.rule_text: The process should generally be assessed for its ability to clear at least three different viruses with differing characteristics.
rules.source_quote: Generally, the process should be assessed for its ability to clear at least three different viruses with differing characteristics.

rules.rule_text: For CHO cell-derived products, the manufacturing process must be assessed for ability to remove and/or inactivate rodent retroviruses; murine leukaemia virus (MLV) may be used as a specific model virus.
rules.source_quote: Cell lines derived from rodents usually contain endogenous retroviruses or RVLPs, which may be infectious (C-type particles) or non-infectious (cytoplasmic A- and R-type particles). The capacity of the manufacturing process to remove and/or inactivate rodent retroviruses from products obtained from such cells should be determined. This can be accomplished by using a murine leukaemia virus—a specific "model" virus in the case of cells of rodent origin.

rules.rule_text: Viral clearance studies must be conducted in a separate laboratory equipped for virological work by staff with virological expertise; it is inappropriate to introduce any unintended virus into a production facility.
rules.source_quote: It is inappropriate to introduce any unintended virus into a production facility because of good manufacturing practice constraints. Therefore, viral clearance studies should be conducted in a separate laboratory equipped for virological work and performed by staff with virological expertise in collaboration with production personnel with experience in the specific process involved in designing and preparing a scale-down model of the purification process.

rules.rule_text: The scale-down model for viral clearance studies must be demonstrated to represent the production process as closely as possible, including representativeness for chromatographic steps.
rules.source_quote: The representativeness of the scale-down model should be demonstrated and should represent the production process as closely as possible. The scale-down model should also consider the robustness of viral clearance (i.e., ability of a process or process step to tolerate variability of materials and changes of the process without negative impact). The performance of the scale-down model should be demonstrated to represent the production process (e.g., comparable yield and purity).

rules.rule_text: Reproducible viral clearance must be confirmed in at least two independent experiments, unless a reduction of studies is justified by prior knowledge.
rules.source_quote: As a general principle, reproducible viral clearance should be confirmed in at least two independent experiments, unless a reduction of studies is justified by prior knowledge (see Section 6.6).

rules.rule_text: For inactivation assessment, studies must include at least one time point less than the minimum exposure time and greater than zero to construct an inactivation curve.
rules.source_quote: It is recommended that studies for inactivation include at least one time point less than the minimum exposure time and greater than zero, in addition to the minimum exposure time. Additional data are particularly important if the virus is a "relevant" virus known to be a human pathogen, and an effective inactivation process is being designed.

rules.rule_text: Whenever significant changes in upstream or downstream manufacturing processes are made, the effect on viral clearance must be considered and the system re-evaluated as needed.
rules.source_quote: Whenever significant changes in the upstream or downstream manufacturing processes are made, the effect of that change, both direct and indirect, on viral clearance should be considered and the system re-evaluated, as needed. For example, changes in upstream manufacturing processes may cause significant changes in the amount of virus produced by the cell line; changes in process steps may change the extent of viral clearance.

rules.rule_text: An effective viral clearance step must generally give reproducible reduction of virus load in the order of 4 log10 or more shown by at least two independent studies.
rules.source_quote: An effective viral clearance step generally gives reproducible reduction of virus load in the order of 4 log10 or more shown by at least two independent studies. However, it is recognised that steps giving a reproducible reduction of 1 to 3 log10 contribute towards viral safety and can be considered for assessment of overall virus reduction.

rules.rule_text: Implementing two distinct viral clearance steps that complement each other in their mode of action is recommended; one should provide effective clearance of non-enveloped virus.
rules.source_quote: It is recommended to design a downstream process that clears a wide range of potential viruses (potential adventitious virus contaminants, endogenous and/or production virus). In this context, whenever feasible and not adversely affecting the product, implementing two distinct viral clearance steps, one of which should provide effective clearance of non-enveloped virus, that complement each other in their mode of action is recommended.

rules.rule_text: A reduction in virus load of less than 1 log10 is considered negligible and should be ignored unless justified.
rules.source_quote: An overall reduction factor is generally expressed as the sum of the individual factors. However, reduction in virus load less than 1 log10 is considered negligible and should be ignored unless justified.

rules.rule_text: The 95% confidence limits for the reduction factor observed should be calculated wherever possible in studies of clearance of relevant and specific model viruses.
rules.source_quote: The 95% confidence limits for the reduction factor observed should be calculated wherever possible in studies of clearance of "relevant" and specific "model" viruses. If the 95% confidence limits for the viral assays of the starting material are +s, and for the viral assays of the material after the step are +a, the 95% confidence limits for the reduction factor are

rules.rule_text: For CHO cells (Case B), a value of ≤10^-4 particles/dose is considered acceptable for retroviral-like particles for mAbs or other recombinant proteins if testing fails to identify the presence of infectious retroviruses.
rules.source_quote: In the Case B scenario for CHO cells, since endogenous retroviruses have been extensively characterised as non-infectious, a value of ≤10-4 particles/dose is considered acceptable for retroviral-like particles for mAbs or other recombinant proteins if testing fails to identify the presence of infectious retroviruses.

rules.rule_text: For platform validation of XMLV inactivation using Triton X-100, a concentration of ≥0.5% Triton X-100 for ≥60 minutes at ≥15°C effectively inactivates XMLV for multiple cell culture derived products.
rules.source_quote: consistent with current process understanding for using Triton X-100 with clarified HCCF, at a concentration of ≥ 0.5% Triton X-100 for ≥ 60 minutes at ≥ 15°C effectively inactivates XMLV for multiple cell culture derived products. When using SD-reagents, treatment with 1% Triton X-100 and 0.3% Tri-N-Butylphosphate for ≥ 30 minutes or treatment with 1% polysorbate 80 and 0.3% Tri-N-Butylphosphate for ≥ 6 hours at ≥ 23°C effectively inactivates retroviruses.

rules.rule_text: For platform validation of low pH inactivation, treatment at ≤pH 3.6, ≥15°C for ≥30 minutes at ≤500 mmol/L sodium chloride concentration effectively inactivates XMLV.
rules.source_quote: Consistent with current process understanding, low pH treatment at ≤ pH 3.6, ≥ 15°C for ≥ 30 minutes at ≤ 500 mmol/L sodium chloride concentration is effectively inactivating XMLV. Acetate and citrate buffer are most commonly used and allow for robust XMLV inactivation.

rules.rule_text: Prior to detergent inactivation, the product intermediate should be clarified from cells/cellular debris including a filtration step of ≤0.2 µm nominal pore size.
rules.source_quote: Therefore, at manufacture, the product intermediate (e.g., Harvested Cell Culture Fluid (HCCF)) should be clarified from cells/cellular debris including a filtration step of ≤0.2 µm nominal pore size before detergent inactivation.

rules.rule_text: For continuous manufacturing (CM), the viral safety strategy must be based on a risk assessment of potential sources of contamination, the ability of the process to remove viruses, and testing capability to assure absence of viruses.
rules.source_quote: The viral safety strategy in CM processes should be based on a risk assessment of potential sources of contamination (e.g., the starting and raw materials and extended cell culture duration), the ability of the process to remove viruses, and the testing capability to assure absence of viruses. Guidance on testing provided in Sections 3 and 4 is also considered applicable to CM.

rules.rule_text: For viral vectors and viral vector-derived products (Annex 6), extensive testing and characterisation for both endogenous viruses and adventitious viruses must be performed at suitable stages of manufacturing.
rules.source_quote: Extensive testing and characterisation for both endogenous viruses and adventitious viruses should be performed at suitable stages of manufacturing to support the overall product safety. Based on the product type and its associated risk factors, the testing scheme should apply across the product lifecycle.

rules.rule_text: For viral vector products, Replication Competent Virus (RCV) testing is recommended at multiple stages of manufacture including on each unprocessed bulk.
rules.source_quote: Replication Competent Virus (RCV) may develop at any stage during manufacturing. Recommendations include testing for RCV at multiple stages of manufacture. RCV testing is performed on the virus seed/bank and on each unprocessed bulk.

rules.rule_text: The absence of production virus must be demonstrated in the drug substance (purified bulk) using an infectivity assay with relevant permissive cell lines for sensitive virus detection, for each purified bulk unless justified by robust excess clearance.
rules.source_quote: Testing for the absence of the production virus should be performed for each purified bulk, unless justified by robust excess clearance (Section 6.3). In this case, absence of residual production virus should be confirmed by testing of at least 3 batches of purified bulk.

rules.rule_text: Adventitious viruses may be introduced into the production process via contaminated biological raw materials, virus seeds, mammalian-derived antibody-coupled affinity resins, contaminated excipients, or contamination from the environment.
rules.source_quote: Adventitious viruses may contaminate the production process by several routes including, but not limited to: (1) the use of contaminated biological raw materials or reagents such as animal serum components during cell culture; (2) the use of a contaminated virus seed for production (see Annex 6); (3) the use of a contaminated raw material or reagent used during downstream purification, such as a mammalian derived antibody-coupled affinity resin for product selection or purification; (4) the use of a contaminated excipient during formulation; and (5) contamination from the environment.

rules.rule_text: Use of human- and animal-derived raw materials should be avoided when possible; when used, the materials must be supported by relevant documentation or qualification of the material commensurate with risk.
rules.source_quote: Manufacturers should avoid using human- and animal-derived raw materials (e.g., human serum, bovine serum, porcine trypsin) in their manufacturing processes when possible. When this is not possible, the use of human- and animal-derived raw materials should be supported by the relevant documentation or qualification of the material, commensurate with risk.

rules.rule_text: The expression construct in the MCB must be analysed for copy number, integration sites, and insertions or deletions using restriction endonuclease mapping or other suitable techniques.
rules.source_quote: Restriction endonuclease mapping or other suitable techniques should be used to analyse the expression construct for copy number, for insertions or deletions, and for the number of integration sites. For extrachromosomal expression systems, the percent of host cells retaining the expression construct should be determined.

rules.rule_text: The protein coding sequence for the recombinant protein product of the expression construct must be verified; for chromosomal copies, verification may be by recloning and sequencing of chromosomal copies.
rules.source_quote: The protein coding sequence for the recombinant protein product of the expression construct should be verified. For extrachromosomal expression systems, the expression construct should be isolated and the nucleotide sequence encoding the product should be verified without further cloning. For cells with chromosomal copies of the expression construct, the nucleotide sequence encoding the product could be verified by recloning and sequencing of chromosomal copies.

rules.rule_text: The nucleic acid sequence must be identical, within the limits of detection of the methodology, to that determined for the expression construct and must correspond to that expected for the protein sequence.
rules.source_quote: The nucleic acid sequence should be identical, within the limits of detection of the methodology, to that determined for the expression construct as described in Section III.A. and should correspond to that expected for the protein sequence.

rules.rule_text: The description of the expression construct assembly steps must include the source and function of the component parts (origins of replication, antibiotic resistance genes, promoters, enhancers) and a detailed component map and complete annotated sequence of the plasmid.
rules.source_quote: The steps in the assembly of the expression construct should be described in detail. This description should include the source and function of the component parts of the expression construct, e.g., origins of replication, antibiotic resistance genes, promoters, enhancers, whether or not the protein is being synthesised as a fusion protein. A detailed component map and a complete annotated sequence of the plasmid should be given.

rules.rule_text: The nucleotide sequence of the coding region of the gene of interest and associated flanking regions that are inserted into the vector, up to and including the junctions of insertion, must be determined by DNA sequencing of the construct.
rules.source_quote: The nucleotide sequence of the coding region of the gene of interest and associated flanking regions that are inserted into the vector, up to and including the junctions of insertion, should be determined by DNA sequencing of the construct.

rules.rule_text: Production of the recombinant protein must be based on well-defined Master and Working Cell Banks; all cell banks must be characterised for relevant phenotypic and genotypic markers.
rules.source_quote: Production of the recombinant protein should be based on well-defined Master and Working Cell Banks. A cell bank is a collection of ampoules of uniform composition stored under defined conditions each containing an aliquot of a single pool of cells. The Master Cell Bank (MCB) is generally derived from the selected cell clone containing the expression construct. The Working Cell Bank (WCB) is derived by expansion of one or more aliquots of the MCB. The cell line history and production of the cell banks should be described in detail, including methods and reagents used during culture, in vitro cell age, and storage conditions. All cell banks should be characterised for relevant phenotypic and genotypic markers which could include the expression of the recombinant protein or presence of the expression construct.

rules.rule_text: The limit for in vitro cell age for production must be based on data derived from production cells expanded under pilot plant scale or full scale conditions to the proposed in vitro cell age or beyond.
rules.source_quote: The limit for in vitro cell age for production should be based on data derived from production cells expanded under pilot plant scale or full scale conditions to the proposed in vitro cell age or beyond. Generally, the production cells are obtained by expansion of the Working Cell Bank; the Master Cell Bank could be used to prepare the production cells with appropriate justification.

rules.rule_text: The expression construct in the production cells must be analysed once for the MCB; the protein coding sequence of the expression construct in the production cells may be verified by nucleic acid testing or analysis of the final protein product.
rules.source_quote: The expression construct of the production cells should be analysed once for the MCB as described in Section III.B., except that the protein coding sequence of the expression construct in the production cells could be verified by either nucleic acid testing or analysis of the final protein product. Increases in the defined limit for in vitro cell age for production should be supported by data from cells which have been expanded to an in vitro cell age which is equal to or greater than the new limit for in vitro cell age.

rules.rule_text: Stability data supporting a requested storage period for either drug substance or drug product must be based on long-term, real-time, real-condition stability studies.
rules.source_quote: Primary data to support a requested storage period for either drug substance or drug product should be based on long-term, real-time, real-condition stability studies. Thus, the development of a proper long-term stability program becomes critical to the successful development of a commercial product.

rules.rule_text: Stability data for drug substance (bulk material) must be provided on at least 3 batches; a minimum of 6 months stability data must be submitted at the time of submission for storage periods greater than 6 months.
rules.source_quote: Where bulk material is to be stored after manufacture but prior to formulation and final manufacturing, stability data should be provided on at least 3 batches for which manufacture and storage are representative of the manufacturing scale of production. A minimum of 6 months stability data at the time of submission should be submitted in cases where storage periods greater than 6 months are requested.

rules.rule_text: Stability information for drug product (final container product) must be provided on at least 3 batches of final container product representative of that which will be used at manufacturing scale.
rules.source_quote: Stability information should be provided on at least 3 batches of final container product representative of that which will be used at manufacturing scale. Where possible, batches of final container product included in stability testing should be derived from different batches of bulk material. A minimum of 6 months data at the time of submission should be submitted in cases where storage periods greater than 6 months are requested.

rules.rule_text: The manufacturer must propose a stability-indicating profile that provides assurance that changes in the identity, purity and potency of the product will be detected.
rules.source_quote: On the whole, there is no single stability-indicating assay or parameter that profiles the stability characteristics of a biotechnological/biological product. Consequently, the manufacturer should propose a stability-indicating profile that provides assurance that changes in the identity, purity and potency of the product will be detected.

rules.rule_text: When potency is linked to a definable and measurable biological activity, testing for potency must be part of the stability studies.
rules.source_quote: When the intended use of a product is linked to a definable and measurable biological activity, testing for potency should be part of the stability studies. For the purpose of stability testing of the products described in this guideline, potency is the specific ability or capacity of a product to achieve its intended effect.

rules.rule_text: Potency study results must be reported in units of biological activity calibrated, whenever possible, against nationally or internationally recognised standard.
rules.source_quote: Potency studies should be performed at appropriate intervals as defined in the stability protocol and the results should be reported in units of biological activity calibrated, whenever possible, against nationally or internationally recognised standard. Where no national or international reference standards exist, the assay results may be reported in in-house derived units using a characterised reference material.

rules.rule_text: The purity of a biotechnological/biological product should be assessed by more than one method; for stability testing purposes, tests for purity should focus on methods for determination of degradation products.
rules.source_quote: For the purpose of stability testing of the products described in this guideline, purity is a relative term. Due to the effect of glycosylation, deamidation, or other heterogeneities, the absolute purity of a biotechnological/biological product is extremely difficult to determine. Thus, the purity of a biotechnological/biological product should be typically assessed by more than one method and the purity value derived is method-dependent. For the purpose of stability testing, tests for purity should focus on methods for determination of degradation products.

rules.rule_text: Limits of acceptable degradation must be derived from the analytical profiles of batches of the drug substance and drug product used in the preclinical and clinical studies.
rules.source_quote: The degree of purity, as well as individual and total amounts of degradation products of the biotechnological/biological product entered into the stability studies, should be reported and documented whenever possible. Limits of acceptable degradation should be derived from the analytical profiles of batches of the drug substance and drug product used in the preclinical and clinical studies.

rules.rule_text: For products with proposed shelf-lives greater than 1 year, stability studies must be conducted every 3 months during the first year of storage, every 6 months during the second year, and annually thereafter.
rules.source_quote: For products with proposed shelf-lives of greater than 1 year, the studies should be conducted every 3 months during the first year of storage, every 6 months during the second year, and annually thereafter.

rules.rule_text: When shelf-lives of 1 year or less are proposed, real-time stability studies must be conducted monthly for the first 3 months and at 3 month intervals thereafter.
rules.source_quote: When shelf-lives of 1 year or less are proposed, the real-time stability studies should be conducted monthly for the first 3 months and at 3 month intervals thereafter.

rules.rule_text: It is strongly suggested that accelerated and stress stability studies be conducted on drug substance and drug product to provide useful support data for establishing the expiration date.
rules.source_quote: However, it is strongly suggested that studies be conducted on the drug substance and drug product under accelerated and stress conditions. Studies under accelerated conditions may provide useful support data for establishing the expiration date, provide product stability information for future product development (e.g., preliminary assessment of proposed manufacturing changes such as change in formulation, scale-up), assist in validation of analytical methods for the stability program, or generate information which may help elucidate the degradation profile of the drug substance or drug product.

rules.rule_text: Each product must retain its specifications within established limits for safety, purity, and potency throughout its proposed shelf-life; specifications and limits must be derived from all available information using the appropriate statistical methods.
rules.source_quote: Each product should retain its specifications within established limits for safety, purity, and potency throughout its proposed shelf-life. These specifications and limits should be derived from all available information using the appropriate statistical methods. The use of different specifications for release and expiration should be supported by sufficient data to demonstrate that clinical performance is not affected as discussed in the tripartite guideline on stability.

rules.rule_text: Sterility testing or alternatives (e.g., container/closure integrity testing) must be performed at a minimum initially and at the end of the proposed shelf-life.
rules.source_quote: Sterility testing or alternatives (e.g., container/closure integrity testing) should be performed at a minimum initially and at the end of the proposed shelf-life.

rules.rule_text: The stability of freeze-dried products after reconstitution must be demonstrated for the conditions and maximum storage period specified on containers, packages, and/or package inserts.
rules.source_quote: The stability of freeze-dried products after their reconstitution should be demonstrated for the conditions and the maximum storage period specified on containers, packages, and/or package inserts. Such labelling should be in accordance with relevant national/regional requirements.

rules.rule_text: The concept of a two-tiered cell bank (MCB/WCB) is generally accepted as the most practical approach for providing a supply of cell substrate; manufacturers must describe their strategy for providing a continued supply of cells.
rules.source_quote: The concept of a two-tiered cell bank, in which the MCB which is used to generate Working Cell Banks (WCBs), is generally accepted as the most practical approach to providing a supply of cell substrate for continued manufacture of the product. Manufacturers should describe their strategy for providing a continued supply of cells from their cell bank(s), including the anticipated utilisation rate of the cell bank(s) for production, the expected intervals between generation of new cell bank(s), and the criteria for qualification of cell bank(s).

rules.rule_text: Manufacturers must prevent a contaminated cell substrate from being used in production; no cell bank testing regimen is able to detect all potential contaminants, therefore preventive principles during cell banking are important.
rules.source_quote: It is important to prevent a contaminated cell substrate (or bank) from being used in production and to avoid a loss of product availability or development time resulting from the need to recreate a cell bank found to be unusable due to contamination. It is recognised that no cell bank testing regimen is able to detect all potential contaminants; therefore, use of these preventive principles during cell banking is important to provide reasonable assurance of the absence of contamination and to provide a reliable source of the cell substrate.

rules.rule_text: Cell bank containers must be traceable and the labelling system must be capable of withstanding the process of preservation, storage, and recovery from storage without loss of labelling information.
rules.source_quote: Manufacturers should describe the procedures used to avoid microbial contamination and cross-contamination by other cell types present in the laboratory, and the procedures that allow the cell bank containers to be traced. This should include a description of the documentation system as well as that of a labelling system which can withstand the process of preservation, storage, and recovery from storage without loss of labelling information on the container.

rules.rule_text: Manufacturers must perform tests for identity and purity once for each MCB, and tests of stability during cell cultivation once for each product to be registered; tests of purity and limited identity tests must be performed once on each WCB.
rules.source_quote: Manufacturers should perform tests for identity and purity once for each MCB, and tests of stability during cell cultivation once for each product to be registered. In addition, tests of purity and limited tests of identity should be performed once on each WCB.

rules.rule_text: Tests for the presence of bioburden (bacteria and fungi) must be performed on individual containers (1% of total number but not less than two containers) of the MCB and WCB.
rules.source_quote: Tests for the presence of bioburden (bacteria and fungi) should be performed on individual containers (1% of the total number but not less than two containers) of the MCB and WCB. In all other aspects, the current methodologies described in either the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.), the Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP) or the U.S. Pharmacopoeia (U.S.P.) for testing microbial limits or microbial sterility may be considered adequate.

rules.rule_text: Tests for the presence of mycoplasma must be performed on the MCB and WCB; current procedures include both agar and broth media procedures as well as the indicator cell culture procedure.
rules.source_quote: Tests for the presence of mycoplasma should be performed on the MCB and WCB. Current procedures considered adequate include both the agar and broth media procedures as well as the indicator cell culture procedure.

rules.rule_text: Virus testing of cell substrates must be designed to detect a wide spectrum of viruses using appropriate screening tests and relevant specific tests, based on the cultivation history of the cell line.
rules.source_quote: Virus testing of cell substrates should be designed to detect a wide spectrum of viruses by using appropriate screening tests and relevant specific tests, based on the cultivation history of the cell line, to detect possible contaminating viruses. Applicants should consult the ICH guideline on viral safety.

rules.rule_text: For evaluation of cell substrate stability during cultivation for production, at least two time points must be examined: one using cells with a minimal number of subcultivations, and another using cells at or beyond the limit of in vitro cell age.
rules.source_quote: For the evaluation of stability during cultivation for production, at least two time points should be examined, one using cells which have received a minimal number of subcultivations, and another using cells at or beyond the limit of in vitro cell age for production use described in the marketing application.

rules.rule_text: For human cell lines, the following characteristics of the original donor must be described: tissue or organ of origin, ethnic and geographical origin, age, sex and general physiological condition; donor's state of health or medical history if known.
rules.source_quote: For human cell lines, it is relevant to describe the following characteristics of the original donor: Tissue or organ of origin, ethnic and geographical origin, age, sex and general physiological condition. If known, the state of health or medical history of the donor should be reported along with the results of any tests of the donor for pathogenic agents.

rules.rule_text: For new or previously uncharacterized diploid cell substrates, confirmation of diploid karyology must be presented and tumorigenic potential must be established from cells from the MCB.
rules.source_quote: For new or previously uncharacterized diploid cell substrates, confirmation of diploid karyology should be presented and tumorigenic potential should be established, using cells from the MCB.

rules.rule_text: For products that are highly purified and contain no cells, karyology and tumorigenicity testing are generally not considered necessary, provided appropriate limits for residual host cell DNA are consistently met.
rules.source_quote: For products that are highly purified and that contain no cells, karyology and tumorigenicity testing are generally not considered necessary, provided that appropriate limits for residual host cell DNA are shown to be consistently met by either process validation studies or by lot release testing.

rules.rule_text: For primary cell substrates used in vaccine production, tissue should be derived from healthy animals subjected to veterinary and laboratory monitoring to certify the absence of pathogenic agents.
rules.source_quote: Information should be provided about the animals used as a source of tissue for the preparation of primary cell substrates. Tissue should be derived from healthy animals subjected to veterinary and laboratory monitoring to certify the absence of pathogenic agents. Whenever possible, donor animals should be obtained from closed, specific pathogen-free (when available) colonies or flocks.

rules.rule_text: The goal of the comparability exercise is to ensure the quality, safety and efficacy of drug product produced by a changed manufacturing process through collection and evaluation of relevant data.
rules.source_quote: The goal of the comparability exercise is to ensure the quality, safety and efficacy of drug product produced by a changed manufacturing process, through collection and evaluation of the relevant data to determine whether there might be any adverse impact on the drug product due to the manufacturing process changes.

rules.rule_text: The demonstration of comparability does not necessarily mean that the quality attributes of pre-change and post-change product are identical, but that they are highly similar and differences have no adverse impact upon safety or efficacy.
rules.source_quote: The demonstration of comparability does not necessarily mean that the quality attributes of the pre-change and post-change product are identical, but that they are highly similar and that the existing knowledge is sufficiently predictive to ensure that any differences in quality attributes have no adverse impact upon safety or efficacy of the drug product.

rules.rule_text: If a manufacturer can provide assurance of comparability through analytical studies alone, nonclinical or clinical studies with the post-change product are not warranted.
rules.source_quote: A determination of comparability can be based on a combination of analytical testing, biological assays, and, in some cases, nonclinical and clinical data. If a manufacturer can provide assurance of comparability through analytical studies alone, nonclinical or clinical studies with the post-change product are not warranted.

rules.rule_text: The product must be evaluated at the process step most appropriate to detect a change in quality attributes; for changes in drug substance manufacture, data on both drug substance and drug product may be appropriate.
rules.source_quote: The goal of the comparability exercise is to ascertain that pre- and post-change drug product is comparable in terms of quality, safety, and efficacy. To meet this goal, the product should be evaluated at the process step most appropriate to detect a change in the quality attributes. This may entail evaluating the product at multiple stages of manufacture. For example, even though all process changes occurred in the manufacture of the drug substance, in cases where the drug product could be impacted by the change, it might be appropriate to collect data on both the drug substance and the drug product to support the determination of comparability.

rules.rule_text: The battery of tests for the comparability exercise must be carefully selected and optimised to maximise the potential for detecting relevant differences in quality attributes.
rules.source_quote: The battery of tests for the comparability exercise should be carefully selected and optimised to maximise the potential for detecting relevant differences in the quality attributes of the product that might result from the proposed manufacturing process change.

rules.rule_text: Methods used to measure quality attributes for batch release must be validated in accordance with ICH guidelines (Q2A, Q2B, Q5C, Q6B).
rules.source_quote: Those methods used to measure quality attributes for batch release should be validated in accordance with ICH guidelines (ICH Q2A, Q2B, Q5C, Q6B), as appropriate.

rules.rule_text: Following a manufacturing process change, manufacturers must attempt to determine that higher order structure (secondary, tertiary, and quaternary) is maintained in the post-change product.
rules.source_quote: Following a manufacturing process change, manufacturers should attempt to determine that higher order structure (secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure) is maintained in the product. If the appropriate higher order structural information cannot be obtained, a relevant biological activity assay (see biological activity below) could indicate a correct conformational structure.

rules.rule_text: New contaminants in the post-change product must be evaluated to assess their potential impact on the quality, safety and efficacy of the product.
rules.source_quote: Contaminants should be strictly avoided and/or suitably controlled with appropriate in-process acceptance criteria or action limits for drug substance or drug product. New contaminants should be evaluated to assess their potential impact on the quality, safety and efficacy of the product.

rules.rule_text: Real-time/real-temperature stability studies must be initiated on the product potentially affected by a manufacturing process change.
rules.source_quote: Therefore, real-time/real temperature stability studies on the product potentially affected by the change should be initiated, as appropriate.

rules.rule_text: For approved products, data from commercial-scale batches is generally indicated to support process changes; the manufacturer must confirm that pre-change and post-change product are comparable.
rules.source_quote: To support the comparison it is often useful to demonstrate, for example, that specific intermediates are comparable or that the modified process has the capability to provide appropriate levels of removal for process- and product-related impurities, including those newly introduced by the process change. To support process changes for approved products, data from commercial-scale batches are generally indicated.

rules.rule_text: For post-change manufacturing process, demonstration of state of control must include appropriate bioburden and/or viral safety testing of the post-change cell banks and cells at the limit of in vitro cell age.
rules.source_quote: Demonstration of state of control with the modified/changed manufacturing process might include, but is not limited to, such items as: Establishment of modified specifications for raw, source and starting materials, and reagents; Appropriate bioburden and/or viral safety testing of the post-change cell banks and cells at the limit of in vitro cell age for production; Adventitious agent clearance; Removal of product- or process-related impurities, such as residual host cell DNA and proteins; and Maintenance of the purity level.

rules.rule_text: During product development, comparability exercises are performed to demonstrate that nonclinical and clinical data generated with pre-change product are applicable to post-change product to support the marketing authorisation.
rules.source_quote: Comparability exercises are generally performed to demonstrate that nonclinical and clinical data generated with pre-change product are applicable to post-change product in order to facilitate further development and, ultimately, to support the marketing authorisation.

rules.rule_text: Additional evidence from nonclinical or clinical studies is considered appropriate when quality data are insufficient to establish comparability; extent and nature of nonclinical and clinical studies must be determined on a case-by-case basis.
rules.source_quote: Additional evidence from nonclinical or clinical studies is considered appropriate when quality data are insufficient to establish comparability. The extent and nature of nonclinical and clinical studies will be determined on a case-by-case basis in consideration of various factors.

rules.rule_text: When differences are identified in quality attributes where a possible adverse impact on safety and efficacy profiles cannot be excluded, the manufacturer should consider performing nonclinical and/or clinical studies.
rules.source_quote: Although the pre- and post-change product appear highly similar, some differences have been identified in the comparison of quality attributes and a possible adverse impact on safety and efficacy profiles cannot be excluded. In such situations, the generation and analysis of additional data on quality attributes are unlikely to assist in determining whether pre- and post-change product are comparable. The manufacturer should consider performing nonclinical and/or clinical studies.

rules.rule_text: ICH Q5C and Q1A(R) must be consulted to determine the conditions for stability studies that provide relevant data to be compared before and after a manufacturing process change.
rules.source_quote: ICH Q5C and Q1A(R) should be consulted to determine the conditions for stability studies that provide relevant data to be compared before and after a change.

rules.rule_text: The characterisation and testing of banked cell substrates is a critical component of the control of biotechnological and biological products; the objective of testing is to confirm identity, purity, and suitability for manufacturing use.
rules.source_quote: The characterisation and testing of banked cell substrates is a critical component of the control of biotechnological and biological products. Characterisation of the MCB allows the manufacturer to assess this source with regard to presence of cells from other lines, adventitious agents, endogenous agents and molecular contaminants (e.g., toxins or antibiotics from the host organism). The objective of this testing is to confirm the identity, purity, and suitability of the cell substrate for manufacturing use.

rules.rule_text: For continuous cell lines of metazoan origin, culture duration should be quantitated by estimation of number of population doublings, number of subcultivations at defined dilution ratio, or time in days.
rules.source_quote: For continuous cell lines of metazoan origin, it is usually adequate to quantitate culture duration by estimation of either number of population doublings, or number of subcultivations at defined dilution ratio, or time in days.

rules.rule_text: To ensure continuous uninterrupted production, manufacturers must consider protection from catastrophic events that could render the cell bank unusable, including redundancy in storage at multiple sites.
rules.source_quote: To ensure continuous, uninterrupted production of pharmaceuticals, manufacturers should carefully consider the steps that can be taken to provide for protection from catastrophic events that could render the cell bank unusable. Examples of these events include fires, power outages and human error. Manufacturers should describe their plans for such precautions; for example, these may include redundancy in the storage of bank containers in multiple freezers, use of back-up power, use of automatic liquid nitrogen fill systems for storage units, storage of a portion of the MCB and WCB at remote sites, or regeneration of the MCB.

rules.rule_text: Virus spike addition in clearance studies must be in a small volume so as not to dilute or change the characteristics of the product; highly pure and concentrated virus stocks are preferred to minimise introduction of non-representative impurities.
rules.source_quote: The virus "spike" should be added to the product in a small volume so as not to dilute or change the characteristics of the product. Using highly pure and concentrated virus stocks for spiking can be helpful to minimise dilution of the product intermediate as well as to reduce introduction of non-representative impurities that may impact process performance.

rules.rule_text: Buffers and product must be evaluated independently for toxicity and interference in assays used to determine the virus titre; if solutions are toxic to indicator cells, dilution, pH adjustment, or dialysis may be necessary.
rules.source_quote: Buffers and product should be evaluated independently for toxicity and interference in assays used to determine the virus titre, as these components may adversely affect the indicator cells. If the solutions are toxic to the indicator cells, dilution, adjustment of the pH, or dialysis of the buffer containing spiked virus might be necessary.

rules.rule_text: Virus reduction factor of an individual removal or inactivation step is defined as the log10 of the ratio of the virus load in the pre-processed material and the virus load in the post-processed material.
rules.source_quote: The virus reduction factor of an individual removal or inactivation step is defined as the log10 of the ratio of the virus load in the pre-processed material and the virus load in the post-processed material that is ready for use in the next step of the process.

rules.rule_text: For platform validation claims, viral clearance claims for a new product based on prior knowledge should include a discussion of all relevant platform data available and rationale to support the platform validation approach.
rules.source_quote: In general, viral clearance claims for a new product based on prior knowledge including in-house experience should include a discussion of all relevant platform data available and the rationale to support the platform validation approach (see Section 6.6). Part of the prior knowledge and in-house data used to reduce product-specific experiments could be provided as a comparison of the new product and its manufacturing process with other in-house products, related process conditions, and process intermediates.

rules.rule_text: An overall summary of the viral safety assessment must be provided; this summary must contain a brief description of all aspects of the viral safety studies and strategies used to prevent virus contamination.
rules.source_quote: Moreover, the approach used to assure viral safety should be explained and justified. In addition to the detailed data, an overall summary of the viral safety assessment should be provided. This summary should contain a brief description of all aspects of the viral safety studies and strategies used to prevent virus contamination.

rules.rule_text: Three principal, complementary approaches must be applied to control the potential viral contamination of biotechnology products: selecting and testing cell lines, assessing viral clearance capacity, and testing the product at appropriate production steps.
rules.source_quote: Three principal, complementary approaches are applied to control the potential viral contamination of biotechnology products: Selecting and testing cell lines and other raw materials, including media components, for the absence of undesirable infectious viruses; Assessing the capacity of the production processes to clear adventitious and endogenous viruses; Testing the product at appropriate steps of production for demonstrating the absence of contaminating infectious viruses.

rules.rule_text: Transmissible spongiform encephalopathy (TSE) associated issues are not in scope of Q5A(R2); applicants are encouraged to discuss TSE-associated issues with the appropriate regulatory authorities.
rules.source_quote: In this document, the term virus excludes nonconventional transmissible agents like those associated with mammalian prions (e.g., bovine spongiform encephalopathy, scrapie). Applicants are encouraged to discuss transmissible spongiform encephalopathy associated issues with the appropriate regulatory authorities as they are not in scope of this guideline.
